issues.title: Incorrect JOIN behavior
issues.line:
LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED'
issues.problem: The LEFT JOIN followed by a WHERE clause on the right table effectively turns it into an INNER JOIN, dropping customers with no completed transactions.
issues.fix: Use INNER JOIN if the intention is to only include customers with completed transactions.

issues.title: Missing GROUP BY column
issues.problem: The query is missing c.tier in the GROUP BY clause, which will cause errors in some SQL databases.
issues.fix: Add c.tier to the GROUP BY clause.

issues.title: PII exposure
issues.problem: Selecting email exposes Personally Identifiable Information (PII), which should be avoided unless necessary.
issues.fix: Remove c.email from the SELECT clause.

issues.title: No date filter
issues.problem: The query lacks a date filter, leading to a full table scan on the fact_transactions table, which can be inefficient.
issues.fix: Add a date filter to limit the data scanned, e.g., WHERE transaction_date >= '2023-01-01'.

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining complex logic or business rules.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of the query and any complex logic.

review.corrected_sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, c.tier,
       SUM(t.amount) as lifetime_value,
       COUNT(t.transaction_id) as total_orders
FROM dim_customer c
INNER JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED'
GROUP BY c.customer_name, c.tier
HAVING SUM(t.amount) > 1000
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC;
review.summary: The query had issues with incorrect JOIN behavior, missing GROUP BY column, PII exposure, lack of date filter, and readability. The corrected query addresses these issues.

issues.title: Correlated subquery
issues.problem: The correlated subquery runs for each row, which is inefficient and can be very slow at scale.
issues.fix: Use a JOIN to avoid the correlated subquery.

issues.title: Alias reference error
issues.problem: The alias 'failed_count' cannot be referenced in the same SELECT clause where it is defined.
issues.fix: Calculate the failure rate in an outer query or a subquery.

issues.title: Integer division
issues.problem: Integer division will truncate the decimal part, leading to incorrect failure rates.
issues.fix: Use decimal division by casting one of the operands to DECIMAL.

issues.title: Potentially missing index
issues.problem: The query could benefit from indexes on transaction_date and status for faster filtering and grouping.
issues.fix: CREATE INDEX idx_fact_transactions_date_status ON fact_transactions(transaction_date, status);

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic, which can make it harder to understand for other developers.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of the query and its logic.

review.corrected_sql:
WITH failed_transactions AS (
  SELECT transaction_date, COUNT(*) AS failed_count
  FROM fact_transactions
  WHERE status = 'FAILED'
  GROUP BY transaction_date
),
total_transactions AS (
  SELECT transaction_date, COUNT(*) AS total_count
  FROM fact_transactions
  GROUP BY transaction_date
)
SELECT t.transaction_date,
       f.failed_count,
       t.total_count,
       CAST(f.failed_count AS DECIMAL(10,2)) / t.total_count * 100 AS failure_rate
FROM total_transactions t
LEFT JOIN failed_transactions f ON t.transaction_date = f.transaction_date
ORDER BY t.transaction_date;
review.summary: The query had several correctness issues, including a correlated subquery and integer division. It also lacked comments for readability and could benefit from performance optimizations.

issues.title: Incorrect revenue calculation
issues.problem: The query does not filter transactions by status 'COMPLETED' for revenue calculation.
issues.fix: SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_revenue

issues.title: Incorrect failure rate calculation
issues.line: ROUND(100.0 * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f3 WHERE f3.merchant_id = m.merchant_id AND f3.status = 'FAILED') / NULLIF(COUNT(t.transaction_id), 0), 2) AS failure_rate_pct
issues.problem: The failure rate calculation does not filter transactions by status 'COMPLETED' for the denominator.
issues.fix: ROUND(100.0 * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f3 WHERE f3.merchant_id = m.merchant_id AND f3.status = 'FAILED') / NULLIF(COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.transaction_id ELSE NULL END), 0), 2) AS failure_rate_pct

issues.title: Correlated subqueries
issues.problem: Correlated subqueries can be inefficient as they are executed for each row in the result set.
issues.fix: Use JOINs instead of correlated subqueries.

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the purpose and logic of each part.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose and logic of each part of the query.

issues.title: Implicit join syntax
issues.line: FROM fact_transactions t, dim_merchant m, dim_customer c
issues.problem: Using implicit join syntax can make the query harder to read and understand.

review.corrected_sql:
-- Merchant Performance Dashboard Query
-- Purpose: Show top merchants by revenue with customer and failure stats

SELECT
    m.merchant_name,
    m.category,
    m.city,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_revenue,

    COUNT(t.transaction_id) AS total_txns,

    (SELECT COUNT(*)
     FROM fact_transactions f2
     WHERE f2.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
       AND f2.status = 'FAILED') AS failed_cnt,

    ROUND(100.0 * (SELECT COUNT(*)
                    FROM fact_transactions f3
                    WHERE f3.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
                      AND f3.status = 'FAILED')
            / NULLIF(COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.transaction_id ELSE NULL END), 0), 2) AS failure_rate_pct,

    c.customer_name,
    c.email,

    SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.transaction_id ELSE NULL END) AS avg_completed_txn_value

FROM fact_transactions t
JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
JOIN dim_customer c ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id

GROUP BY m.merchant_name, m.category, m.city, c.customer_name, c.email

ORDER BY total_revenue ASC;
review.summary: The query had critical correctness issues with revenue and failure rate calculations. It also had performance and readability issues with correlated subqueries and implicit joins.

issues.title: Missing COMPLETED status filter
issues.problem: The query includes all transaction statuses in the revenue calculation, not just 'COMPLETED'.

issues.title: Implicit JOIN syntax
issues.problem: Using implicit JOIN syntax can lead to less readable and maintainable code, and can sometimes cause performance issues.
issues.fix: FROM fact_transactions t JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining its purpose and logic, which can make it harder for others to understand.
issues.fix: Add comments at the top of the query explaining its purpose and logic.

issues.title: ORDER BY direction
issues.problem: The revenue is ordered in ascending order, but typically the top merchants by revenue would be ordered in descending order.

review.corrected_sql:
-- revenue_by_merchant.sql
-- Calculates the total revenue and transaction count for each merchant,
-- only including transactions with a status of 'COMPLETED'.

SELECT m.merchant_name,
       SUM(t.amount) as total_revenue,
       COUNT(*) as txn_count
FROM fact_transactions t
JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
WHERE t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
AND t.status = 'COMPLETED'
AND t.transaction_date > '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY m.merchant_name
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 10;
review.summary: The query had issues with missing filters, implicit JOIN syntax, readability, and ORDER BY direction. Corrected and improved for better performance and readability.
